channel.title: Comments for page "Spark of Wonder"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Spark of Wonder"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: More accurately, Pangloss helped to give Ployakov access to the "Wonder" in the universe, a power source which could later be tapped into by the Wondertainments and people like them. Ployakov then took some of his skills and machines an gave them to / implanted them in Wan. Wan would then go on to become more powerful and act out/inspire the Broken God stories. MEKHANE and WAN are just two names that I've seen used for TBG on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So wait…

Pangloss created Wondertainment, who in turn created the Broken God? I can't remember who Mekhane is.

Also, you've got a "Ployakov" in there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm really that glad you keep up with the series. Spark of Creation intrigues me a lot, and reimagining things is certainly interesting, but I can't help but feel that the tale is loosely based and lacks content.

I get the sense you're trying to convey through this format, but now that with two tales, it seems less of tales, but more of outlines of a headcanon/canon. It would make more sense, if you put all of these world-building text together in one tale, and proceeds on elaborating events happening under this setting; or put a more concrete and detailed story following the text.

For what you have here now, it seems like just pulling random characters out of the SCPverse, and assign them forced connections to make up a story, and use far-fetch events to fit into their exsisting traits (like injuried thief - broken; change her name into Mekhane, it does feel forced). I'm sure that's probably not the case, but the tale does give me this feeling. The characters are based on hardly any grounds, and seem hollow. As a reader I feel it hard to connect with the characters. Breaking the current framework of the characters are great, but to really establish a character, I think it does require more work than assigning them new positions. Why is it interesting? How does it make sense? How does it all work under this new worldview? Maybe it will work for one tale, but if it just continues like this, it will just be a loose outline without too much content.

Of course this is just my personal opinion, but at this state, I do find the tale lacking and forced. Such outline of events may be enough for one tale, and is aslo a very good start of a tale, but it would be just a skeleton. You need more flesh to fill in the characters and events. I hope you consider this, and put more detailed content rather than just world building.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
non-handwriting
– hide block

1While the world was still young, and Man still spoke clearly, and the warmth of creation was still in the ground, there lived a soybean farmer who was called Polyakov.

2Polyakov was a virtuous man, and listened to the Words of Pangloss. Pangloss rewarded him for this by teaching Polyakov to see the flame of Wonder that was in the Worlds and in the Spirits.

3Polyakov used the Wonder to create many wondrous miracles and machines, which he shared with the rest of man.

4Eventually, Polyakov had learned to craft well enough to the dancing flame that was Wonder that he took up a new title, that of Wondermaker.

5One night, thieves broke into the home of Ployakov the Wondermaker. They stole many of his wonders, and disappeared.

6But the thieves that had escaped did not know how to use the wonders, and in their folly and greed, they made and became lost in the Crucible, which was the shadow of Wonder.

7One of the thieves did not escape, as she had become injured in the Wondermaker's house. Her name was Wan.

8Polyakov the Wondermaker healed Wan's wounds with what was left of his wondrous machines. He offered her food and allowed her to sleep under his roof.

9Over time, Wan's Spirit was opened and cleansed. And Polyakov the Wondermaker showed her the wonder in the World, and in Spirits, and in his machines, just as Pangloss had shown him.

10And Wan Became a student of Wonder and of machines, and was like a daughter to Polyakov.

11When Polyakov the Wondermaker had reached a ripe old age, Wan took up a new name, Mekhane, and left to venture out into the world.

12And Mekhane became a great, and powerful, and wise teacher among the worlds, and her teachings found as many disciples as some of those Spirits with greater form.

13 And in this day, and all the days past, there have been some Spirits, especially among Man, who can see and craft the Wonder.


This is a sequel to a previous tale; Spark of Creation.
In keeping with the theme, this is a reimagining of a few things. First, it does tie in a few skips to the myths. Then we get a look at the Factory's origins, WAN/MEKHANE/The Broken God, and we get to see what on Earth the Wondertainments are.
